items.mistake: Using `d[k]` to read a possibly-missing key.
items.fix: Use `d.get(k, default)` (or `k in d` first) to avoid `KeyError`.
items.why: Indexing raises when the key is absent; `get` lets you supply a safe fallback.

items.mistake: Using a mutable object (list, dict, set) as a key.
items.fix: Use an immutable, hashable key (str, int, tuple of hashables, or a frozen dataclass).
items.why: Mutable values can change after insertion, breaking the invariant that `hash(k)` stays consistent with `==`.
items.example:
```python
key = ("chat", model, temperature)   # tuple is hashable
cache[key] = response
```

items.mistake: Indexing a `defaultdict` just to test membership.
items.fix: Use `k in d` or `d.get(k)` — only index when you actually want to write.
items.why: Indexing a defaultdict triggers the default factory and inserts the key as a side effect.
items.example:
```python
d = defaultdict(list)
'x' in d        # False (no side effect)
d['x']          # creates [] and inserts 'x'!
```

items.mistake: Scanning a list to find an item by id every time.
items.fix: Build a dict keyed by id once and look up in O(1).
items.why: List scans are O(n) per lookup; repeated lookups become O(n·m). A dict turns each into O(1).
items.example:
```python
by_id = {d.id: d for d in docs}
meta  = by_id[chunk.doc_id]   # O(1)
```
